title: Whakapapa of the Flax: Weaving Stories from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Whakapapa o te Harakeke: Te Raranga Kōrero mai i Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: This lesson is rooted in the specific history and environment of our rohe. Students will explore the traditional practices of harvesting and weaving harakeke (flax) along the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant local waterway. They will connect this to the story of a renowned local Ngāti Kahungunu weaver, Nanny Rina, learning how the protocols of harvesting (such as the karakia for the first cut and never taking the 'rito' - the central baby shoot) are a physical expression of kaitiakitanga. This deepens into understanding how these practices and their associated stories are a form of narrative, passed down through generations.
learningObjectives: Analyze and explain the symbolic meaning behind at least three protocols of harakeke harvesting, connecting them to the concept of kaitiakitanga.
Compose a first-person narrative from the perspective of a weaver at Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, accurately incorporating sensory details and specific cultural references.
Collaboratively create and present a 'Living Library' exhibit that showcases a harvested harakeke leaf and its accompanying written and oral story.

activities.title: Te Huarahi o te Kaitiaki | The Kaitiaki's Path
activities.description: A guided visit to the harakeke patches along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. A local kaumātua or weaver will demonstrate the tikanga of harvesting, reciting the karakia and explaining the reasoning behind each action. Students will use sensory language journals to record what they see, hear, smell, and feel, building a vocabulary bank for their writing.

activities.title: He Kōrero mō Nanny Rina | The Story of Nanny Rina
activities.description: Students will read a transcribed oral history or watch a short video interview with a local kuia (like Nanny Rina) discussing the significance of weaving in their life and for the community. In groups, they will identify key themes (whakapapa, identity, guardianship) and the narrative structure of her story.

activities.title: Te Raranga Kōrero | Weaving the Story
activities.description: Using their sensory journals and the themes from Nanny Rina's story, students will draft a first-person narrative. The narrative will be from the perspective of someone learning to harvest harakeke at the awa, focusing on conveying the emotional and cultural significance of the experience through precise word choice.

activities.title: Te Whare Pukapuka Ora | The Living Library
activities.description: A culminating sharing event. Students will work in small groups to create an exhibit for a 'Living Library'. Each exhibit will feature a properly harvested harakeke leaf, the final written narrative, and a brief oral presentation where students take turns explaining the story and its cultural importance to visitors (other classes, whānau).

resources: A local kaumātua or weaver from the Ngāti Kahungunu iwi with knowledge of harakeke tikanga
Digital access to the 'Ngā Tipu Whakaoranga' (Māori Plant Use) database from Manaaki Whenua Landcare Research
The book 'Te Mahi Kete: Māori Basketry for Beginners' by Mick Pendergrast
Sensory kits for the field trip (clipboards, paper, pencils, magnifying glasses)

tasks: Completed sensory language journal from the field trip
Draft and final copy of the first-person narrative
Contribution to the group's 'Living Library' exhibit and oral presentation

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies: Integrates sources of information and prior knowledge to make sense of a text.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Ideas: Selects and develops ideas in a range of texts. Language features: Uses a range of language features appropriately for effect.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Structure: Organises texts effectively.
Social Sciences: Understand how people pass on and sustain culture and heritage for different reasons and that this has consequences for people.
